message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We recently showed that seasonal patterns of COVID-19 incidence and Influenza-Like Illnesses incidence are highly similar, in a country in the temperate climate zone, such as the Netherlands (latitude: 52
                    <jats:sup>°</jats:sup>
                    N). We hypothesize that in The Netherlands the same environmental factors and mobility trends that are associated with the seasonality of flu-like illnesses are predictors of COVID-19 seasonality as well.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We used meteorological, pollen/hay fever and mobility data from the Netherlands with its 17.4 million inhabitants. For the reproduction number of COVID-19 (R
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    ), we used data from the Dutch State Institute for Public Health. This R
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    metric is a daily estimate that is based on positive COVID-19 tests in the Netherlands in hospitals and municipalities. For all datasets we selected the overlapping period of COVID-19 and the first allergy season: from February 17, 2020 till September 21, 2020 (total number of measurements: n = 218), the end of pollen season. Backward stepwise multiple linear regression was used to develop an environmental prediction model of the R
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    of COVID-19. Next, we studied whether adding mobility trends to an environmental model improved the predictive power.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    By means of stepwise backward multiple linear regression four highly significant (p value < 0.01) predictive factors are selected in our combined model: temperature, solar radiation, hay fever incidence, and mobility to indoor recreation locations. Our combined model explains 87.5% of the variance of R
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    of COVID-19 and has a good and highly significant fit: F(4, 213) = 374.2, p-value < 0.00001. The combined model had a better overall predictive performance compared to a solely environmental model, which still explains 77.3% of the variance of R
                    <jats:sub>t</jats:sub>
                    , and a good and highly significant fit: F
                    <jats:sub>(4, 213)</jats:sub>
                    = 181.3, p < 0.00001.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We conclude that the combined mobility and environmental model can adequately predict the seasonality of COVID-19 in a country with a temperate climate like the Netherlands. In this model higher solar radiation, higher temperature and hay fever are related to lower COVID-19 reproduction, and mobility to indoor recreation locations with increased COVID-19 spread.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Highlights</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>The seasonality of COVID-19 can be well-explained by environmental factors and mobility.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>A combined model explains 87.5% of the variance of the reproduction number of COVID-19</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Inhibitors of the reproduction number of COVID-19 are higher solar radiation, and seasonal allergens/allergies.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Mobility, especially to indoor recreation locations, increases the reproduction number of COVID-19.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Temperature has no direct effect on the reproduction number of COVID-19, but affects mobility and seasonal allergens.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Adding mobility trends to an environmental model improves the predictive value regarding the reproduction number of COVID-19.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Environmental factors and mobility predict COVID-19 seasonality

reference.unstructured: Grech V , Cuschieri S , Gauci C. COVID-19: The possible seasonal shape of things to come. Early Hum Dev. 2020 Nov 12: 105262, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.earlhumdev.2020.105262.
Moriyama M , Hugentobler WJ , Iwasaki A. Seasonality of Respiratory Viral Infections. Annual Review of Virology. 2020;7:1, https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-virology-012420-022445.

Sajadi MM , Habibzadeh P , Vintzileos A , Shokouhi S , Miralles-Wilhelm F , Amoroso A. Temperature, Humidity and Latitude Analysis to Predict Potential Spread and Seasonality for COVID-19. 2020 March 5, https://doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.3550308.
Poole L. Seasonal Influences On The Spread Of SARS-CoV-2 (COVID19), Causality, and Forecastabililty (3-15-2020) (March 15, 2020), https://doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.3554746.
